perturbation.original_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that for a period (the "Restrictive Period") which shall commence on the Effective Date of the Merger and shall expire on a date which shall be the latest to occur of: (a) such date as such Stockholder shall no longer be employed on a full-time basis with AUGI or any other subsidiary or affiliate of AUGI; or (b) three (3) years following the effectiveness of the Merger (the "Restrictive Period"), without the prior written consent of AUGI, such Stockholder shall not invest, carry on, engage or become involved, either as an employee, agent, advisor, officer, director, stockholder, manager, partner, joint venturer, participant or consultant in any business enterprise (other than Mergerco, AUGI or any of their respective subsidiaries, affiliates, successors or assigns) which derives any material revenues from the offer or sale in the United States from time to time during the Restrictive Period of services relating to all aspects of providing site acquisition, zoning, architectural and engineering consulting and design services to the wireless telecommunications industry (the "Business"), or which engages in any other business substantially similar to and directly competitive with the Business conducted by the Company as at the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that for a period (the "Restrictive Period") which shall commence on the Effective Date of the Merger and shall expire on a date which shall be the latest to occur of: (a) such date as such Stockholder shall no longer be employed on a full-time basis with AUGI or any other subsidiary or affiliate of AUGI; or (b) ten (10) years following the effectiveness of the Merger (the "Restrictive Period"), without the prior written consent of AUGI, such Stockholder shall not invest, carry on, engage or become involved, either as an employee, agent, advisor, officer, director, stockholder, manager, partner, joint venturer, participant or consultant in any business enterprise (other than Mergerco, AUGI or any of their respective subsidiaries, affiliates, successors or assigns) which derives any material revenues from the offer or sale in the United States from time to time during the Restrictive Period of services relating to all aspects of providing site acquisition, zoning, architectural and engineering consulting and design services to the wireless telecommunications industry (the "Business"), or which engages in any other business substantially similar to and directly competitive with the Business conducted by the Company as at the date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text extends the non-compete period to ten years, which may be deemed unreasonably long and unenforceable under Delaware law, especially if it lacks a reasonable geographic scope or places undue hardship on the employee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding enforceability of non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration. A ten-year restriction may be considered unreasonable, particularly if it prevents the employee from earning a livelihood in their field of expertise, thus conflicting with general Delaware contract law principles requiring reasonable limitations on competition.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 19 > Chapter 7 Authenticated PDF EMPLOYMENT PRACTICES Subchapter I. General Provisions Subchapter II. Discrimination in Employment Subchapter III. Persons With Disabilities Employment Protections Subchapter IV. Right to Inspect Personnel Files Subchapter V. Employment First Act Subchapter VI. Jamie Wolfe Employment Act
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the non-compete period to ten years, while the original text stipulated three years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that under Delaware law, a ten-year non-compete period may be considered unreasonable, especially if it unduly restricts the employee's ability to earn a livelihood. The scraped law snippet mentions 'EMPLOYMENT PRACTICES', indicating that employment-related matters fall under Delaware law. Although the snippet does not provide the specific law about the reasonableness of non-compete agreements, the change introduces a significant extension to the restrictive period which, according to the explanations provided, is likely to be viewed as unreasonable under Delaware law, therefore a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Each of Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.changed_text: Each of Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, for a period of six months following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality period to six months may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, particularly in industries where the information remains valuable for a longer period. This potentially contradicts the Delaware Uniform Trade Secrets Act (DUTSA), which aims to protect trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: DUTSA protects trade secrets as long as they maintain economic value by not being generally known. Reducing the confidentiality period to six months could allow for the disclosure of trade secrets before their value expires, undermining the protections afforded by DUTSA, especially if the information legitimately constitutes a trade secret with a longer lifespan.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction by limiting the confidentiality period to six months, potentially undermining the protection of trade secrets under DUTSA. The original text provides for confidentiality at 'any time following the effectiveness of the Merger,' while the changed text restricts this to six months. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'Trade secret' shall mean information...that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known.' Limiting the confidentiality period to six months could allow for the disclosure of trade secrets before their value expires if the trade secret's economic value persists beyond this period, thus contradicting the aim of DUTSA to protect trade secrets as long as they maintain economic value through secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that a Stockholder is requested pursuant to subpoena or other legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, such Stockholder will provide the Company with prompt notice (unless notice is prohibited by law) so that the Company may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with Section 2 of this Agreement. In the event that such protective order or other remedy is not obtained or that the Company waives compliance with the provisions of Section 2 of this Agreement, the Stockholder will furnish only that portion of the Confidential Information which is legally required.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that a Stockholder is requested pursuant to subpoena or other legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, such Stockholder will disclose the information immediately, regardless of whether the Company is notified or can seek a protective order.
perturbation.explanation: This change requires immediate disclosure regardless of legal protections, potentially violating the attorney-client privilege if the confidential information is protected under that privilege and the company has not had the opportunity to assert it. This contradicts Delaware Rule of Evidence 502.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Rule of Evidence 502 regarding attorney-client privilege.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Rule of Evidence 502 protects attorney-client privilege. The modified text compels disclosure irrespective of the company's ability to claim this privilege or seek a protective order. Therefore, it conflicts with the protections guaranteed by Rule 502.
perturbation.justification: The modified text mandates immediate disclosure of confidential information, regardless of whether the company is notified or can seek a protective order. This directly conflicts with Delaware Rule of Evidence 502, as it removes the opportunity for the company to assert attorney-client privilege, potentially violating its protections.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED (BOTH AS TO VALIDITY AND PERFORMANCE) AND ENFORCED IN ACCORDANCE WITH AND GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE APPLICABLE TO AGREEMENTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY WITHIN SUCH JURISDICTION.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED (BOTH AS TO VALIDITY AND PERFORMANCE) AND ENFORCED IN ACCORDANCE WITH AND GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA APPLICABLE TO AGREEMENTS MADE AND TO BE PERFORMED WHOLLY WITHIN SUCH JURISDICTION.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California might invalidate certain provisions that are valid under Delaware law but not under California law, especially regarding non-compete agreements. This contradicts the principle of respecting the parties' choice of law, assuming the agreement has a reasonable relationship with Delaware.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. Because the contract includes a non-compete clause, changing the governing law to California will likely invalidate this clause as it is against public policy as declared by the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from Delaware law to California law directly contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. As stated in snippet 1, BPC 16600(a) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." This means that the non-compete clause, if present, becomes unenforceable, therefore the changed text creates a contradiction and a legal issue. Furthermore, 16600(b)(1) clarifies that this applies to "any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter."

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder).
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Stockholders severally agrees that such Stockholder shall not, at any time following the effectiveness of the Merger, print, publish, divulge or communicate to any person, firm, corporation or other business organization or use for his or their own account any business contacts, customers, suppliers, technology, product designs and specifications, know-how, trade secrets, marketing techniques, promotional materials or other secret or confidential information relating to the Business or any other businesses then being engaged in by AUGI or any of its subsidiaries, or any secret or confidential information relating to the affairs, dealings and concerns of the Company, AUGI or any of the subsidiaries of AUGI (collectively, the "Confidential Information") which the Stockholder has received or obtained while an employee or member of the Board of Directors of AUGI or the Company (whether or not developed, devised, or otherwise created in whole or in part by the efforts of the Stockholder), even if that information reveals illegal activity on the part of AUGI.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text compels silence even when the confidential information reveals illegal activity. This may violate whistleblower protection laws, which protect individuals who report illegal conduct. This creates a potential conflict with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act if the company is publicly traded and the information relates to securities fraud.
perturbation.law_explanation: SOX prohibits retaliation against whistleblowers who report securities law violations. By requiring the Stockholders to remain silent even about illegal activities, the modified agreement could be interpreted as an attempt to prevent them from reporting securities fraud, violating the protections granted under 18 U.S.C. § 1514A.

Civil action to protect against retaliation in fraud cases (a) Whistleblower Protection for Employees of Publicly Traded Companies .-No company with a class of securities registered under section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 ( 15 U.S.C. 78l ), or that is required to file reports under section 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 ( 15 U.S.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an explicit contradiction with whistleblower protection laws, specifically the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. The added clause mandates silence even when confidential information reveals illegal activity. 18 U.S.C. § 1514A, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1, explicitly provides a "Civil action to protect against retaliation in fraud cases" for employees of publicly traded companies. The modified contract language attempts to prevent stockholders from reporting potential securities fraud, which would violate the protections granted under this law. The original text protected confidential information, but the modified version extends this protection even to illegal activities, directly undermining whistleblower protections.
